short.headline: 61.8 crore Indians are in the workforce, but only 55.7% of adults participate.
short.dek: India’s labour force has doubled since 1990, but participation lags behind neighbours, and unemployment hits youth and graduates hardest.
short.body: At last count, India’s labour force stood at 61.8 crore people, double the 30.8 crore in 1990. But only 55.7% of adults aged 15 and above are actually in the workforce. The rest are not working or looking for work. Among those who are in the labour force, the official unemployment rate is 4.2%, down from 7.6% in 1991. However, these headline numbers mask wide gaps: joblessness among youth (15–29) is 10.2%, graduates face 13% unemployment, and women’s participation, though rising, remains at just 41.7% compared with 78.8% for men. Most workers are self-employed (58.4%), and agriculture still accounts for 43.5% of all jobs. This page unpacks the numbers behind India’s huge and unequal workforce.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: India has one of the youngest populations on Earth, and every year lakhs of young people reach working age. But the data shows that getting a job is not easy, especially if you are a woman, a graduate, or from a Scheduled Caste or Tribe. The good news is that more people are working now than ever before, and unemployment has come down from the scary levels of the late 2010s. The bad news is that more than half of all workers are self-employed, many as street vendors or small farmers, and regular, salaried jobs with benefits are still a small slice. The question is less about ‘how many are working’ and more about ‘what kind of work they get’.
macha.soWhat: India is creating many workers, but not enough good jobs.

article.title: India’s workforce: 61.8 crore and counting, but jobs remain hard to find
article.standfirst: The labour force has doubled in three decades, yet participation is low, unemployment hits the young and educated, and most work is informal. Here is what the data says about who works, who doesn’t, and why.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How many Indians are in the workforce?

India’s labour force, the total number of people aged 15 and above who are either working or actively looking for work, stood at 61.8 crore in 2025. That is double the 30.8 crore counted in 1990. This number includes everyone from daily-wage labourers to corporate executives, as long as they are part of the workforce. It does not include those who have stopped searching for a job or are not interested in paid work. The steady rise over three decades reflects a growing population and a larger share of adults choosing to enter the labour force. However, being in the labour force is not the same as having a job; roughly 4.2% of them cannot find work.

## How many of India’s adults are actually in the workforce?

The labour force participation rate (LFPR) tells us what percentage of the working-age population (15+) is in the labour force. In India, this rate was 55.7% in 2025, according to modelled estimates from the World Bank. That means for every 100 adults, about 56 are either working or job-hunting. The rate has moved in a U-shape: it started at 57.9% in 1990, dipped in the 2000s, and then recovered gradually. A lower participation rate can signal that more people are studying longer, retiring earlier, or, as is often the case for women, remaining outside the formal workforce due to care responsibilities. The recent recovery, however, is partly driven by more women joining the labour force, though some of this may reflect distress rather than opportunity.

## Is being in the workforce the same as having a job?

Not quite. India’s official surveys, the Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS), distinguish between those in the labour force (LFPR) and those actually working (the Worker Population Ratio, or WPR). In 2023-24, the LFPR for persons aged 15 and above was 60.1%, while the WPR was 58.2%. The gap, 1.9 percentage points, is the unemployment rate according to the PLFS. So out of every 100 adults, about 60 wanted work, but only about 58 had it. This gap has narrowed from 3 percentage points in 2017-18, when LFPR was 49.8% and WPR 46.8%, indicating an improvement in job availability. But it also means there are still lakhs of jobseekers unable to find work.

## What is the unemployment rate, and how has it changed?

The unemployment rate is the share of the labour force that is actively seeking work but cannot find a job. The World Bank’s modelled estimate for India stood at 4.2% in 2025, down from 7.6% in 1991. The PLFS, which is India’s own large-scale household survey, recorded a lower rate of 3.2% in 2023-24, compared with 6% in 2017-18. Both sources show a downward trend, but the official rate hides underemployment, people doing part-time or low-productivity work because they cannot find better jobs, and those too discouraged to search. The headline figure, therefore, understates the difficulty many Indians face in securing adequate employment.

## Why is there such a large gap between men and women in the workforce?

The gender gap in workforce participation is stark. In 2023-24, the male LFPR was 78.8%, meaning nearly 4 out of 5 working-age men are in the labour force. For women, the rate was 41.7%, about half that of men. However, women’s participation has risen sharply from just 23.3% in 2017-18. This 18.4 percentage point jump is one of the most significant labour market stories in recent years. But the rise may be double-edged: it partly reflects increased availability of self-employment and casual work, often in agriculture, rather than formal, well-paid jobs. Men’s participation has been relatively stable, edging up from 75.8%. The gap, while narrowing, remains a defining feature of India’s workforce.

## Why do young Indians struggle to find jobs?

Young people face a much tougher job market than older adults. The unemployment rate for persons aged 15–29 was 10.2% in 2023-24, more than three times the overall PLFS rate of 3.2%. Young men fared slightly better (9.8%) than young women (11%). All these rates have fallen sharply from the peak of 2017-18, when youth unemployment touched 17.8% overall. The decline is encouraging, but a double-digit unemployment rate for the young means that millions are entering adulthood without stable work. This age group is also broader than the international standard (15–24), so comparisons with other countries need caution. The persistence of high youth joblessness points to a mismatch between the skills young Indians acquire and the jobs available.

## Why does unemployment rise with education?

One of the most puzzling labour market patterns in India is that higher education does not guarantee lower unemployment. In 2023-24, the unemployment rate for those who were not literate was just 0.2%, while for graduates it was 13%, and for postgraduates and above, 12.4%. Those with secondary education had an unemployment rate of 1.9%. This might seem counterintuitive, but it reflects the fact that the less educated often take any available work, however precarious, because they cannot afford to remain jobless. Graduates and postgraduates, on the other hand, typically seek formal, salaried jobs that match their qualifications, and such jobs are in short supply. The result is an ‘educated unemployed’ phenomenon, where aspiration overshoots opportunity.

## Does unemployment affect all social groups equally?

Unemployment rates vary across social groups, though the gaps have narrowed. In 2023-24, the rate for Scheduled Castes (SC) was 3.3%, for Scheduled Tribes (ST) it was 1.9%, for Other Backward Classes (OBC) it was 3.1%, and for Others it was 3.8%. All were down from 5–7% in 2017-18. The low unemployment rate for ST households may not signify abundant jobs; rather, many ST workers are engaged in agriculture or forest-based self-employment where joblessness is disguised. Similarly, lower rates for some marginalised groups could mask underemployment or distress-driven self-employment. The ‘Others’ category, which includes general castes, shows a slightly higher unemployment rate, partly because of higher education levels and different job expectations.

## Are Indians getting good jobs?

The quality of employment matters as much as the quantity. In 2023-24, 58.4% of workers were self-employed, up from 52.2% in 2017-18. Self-employment spans a wide range: from prosperous entrepreneurs to vulnerable own-account workers like street hawkers. Only 21.7% of workers had regular wage or salaried jobs, the kind that typically come with contracts, social security, and steady pay. This share has slightly fallen from 22.8%. Casual labourers, who work on daily wages without security, made up 19.8% of workers, down from 24.9%. The shift away from casual work is positive, but the growth in self-employment, much of it low-earning, suggests that many Indians are creating their own work because formal jobs are scarce.

## Which sectors do Indians work in?

The sectoral distribution of workers shows a slow transformation. Agriculture remains the largest employer: 43.5% of workers were engaged in it in 2023-24, nearly unchanged from 42.4% in 2017-18. This high share often indicates hidden underemployment, as many farmers and labourers could be absorbed into more productive sectors. Industry (including manufacturing and construction) employed 24.9%, exactly the same share as six years earlier. Services accounted for 31.6%, a slight dip from 32.6%. Contrary to expectations of rapid structural change, the movement of workers from farm to non-farm has been sluggish. Periodic economic shocks can even push workers back into agriculture, which acts as a fallback.

## How does India’s workforce participation compare globally?

India’s labour force participation rate of 55.7% in 2025 is lower than most of its Asian peers. Vietnam leads with 72.8%, followed by Indonesia (68%), China (64.6%), and Bangladesh (58.8%). The world average is 61%. India’s LFPR has historically been lower than China’s, which has declined sharply from 79.2% in 1990. One visible pattern in this data is the extremely low participation of women. In Vietnam and Indonesia, female LFPR is much higher, pulling up the overall rate. Bangladesh is closer to India, also with low female participation, though recent gains in the garment industry have boosted it. These comparisons highlight that India’s workforce participation is not just about economic growth but also about social norms and access to acceptable work.

## How many young Indians are neither working nor studying?

The share of youth not in employment, education, or training (NEET) is a broader measure of disengagement. In 2024, 24.2% of young Indians (aged 15–24 by global definition) were NEET, down from 32.6% in 1994. That is a improvement, but it still means one in four young people are disconnected from both learning and earning. The NEET rate is often higher for young women, many of whom are engaged in unpaid household work. This indicator captures those who are not even looking for jobs, so they are not counted as unemployed, but are also not building skills. India’s NEET rate remains one of the highest in the world, pointing to a large pool of underutilized youth.

## What is the hiring trend telling us right now?

Official employment data is released with a lag, but the Naukri JobSpeak index offers a real-time glimpse into formal sector hiring. The index aggregates job listings on Naukri.com, a major online recruitment platform. In May 2026, the index stood at 2,836, more than triple its level of 902 in August 2008. It shows long-term growth punctuated by sharp drops during the pandemic and demonetisation. Because it is based on online postings, the index skews towards IT, BPO, and urban white-collar jobs. It does not capture hiring in small firms or the informal sector. Still, the trend suggests that demand for formal workers has expanded significantly over the past decade, with occasional setbacks.

## What does the demand for NREGA work tell us about rural jobs?

The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA) provides a legal guarantee of 100 days of wage employment per year to rural households. The person-days of work created under this scheme serve as a rough indicator of rural distress. In April 2026, 109.93 million person-days were generated, down from the peak of 186.84 million in April 2013. NREGA demand spiked dramatically during the COVID-19 lockdowns as millions of migrant workers returned to villages and non-farm work dried up. Since then, it has moderated but remains elevated. High demand can signal both lack of alternative employment and better implementation. Seasonal patterns are strong, with work concentrated in the lean agricultural months.

## How much do different jobs pay?

Earnings differ starkly by employment type. In 2023-24, regular wage or salaried workers earned an average of ₹20,702 per month, up from ₹16,527 in 2017-18. Self-employed workers averaged ₹13,279 per month, a modest rise from ₹12,029. Casual labourers are paid by the day: they earned ₹418 per day on average, up from ₹256. However, casual workers do not find work every day; their monthly earnings are much lower if they are employed only 10–15 days a month. The wide gap between regular and casual earnings shows why a ‘job’ is not a uniform concept. Regular wage jobs come with stability and benefits, while casual labour remains precarious and poorly paid.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India’s workforce numbers are vast: 61.8 crore people are either working or looking for work. This data tracks the size of the labour force, who participates, who gets left out, and what kind of work is available, using the latest surveys and high-frequency indicators.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of it as the pool of people who are either working or actively looking for work. It includes the employed and the unemployed, but not those who have stopped searching or are not interested in working. For example, a housewife who is not seeking paid work is not in the labour force. A student who takes a part-time job is in it. The labour force is the total supply of workers available to produce goods and services.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This page tracks how big this pool is and who is in it, which is the starting point for understanding jobs in India.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine you take everyone aged 15 and above in a village. If 55 of them are either working or looking for work, the LFPR is 55%. It tells you what share of the adult population is economically active. The other 45 might be students, homemakers, retirees, or people too discouraged to search. A low LFPR can mean many people are staying out of the workforce by choice or because of barriers.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It tells us how much of India’s potential workers are actually joining the workforce. A falling LFPR can mean more people are quitting job search.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: If you take 100 people in the labour force, those working or looking for work, and 3 of them cannot find a job, the unemployment rate is 3%. It is not the percentage of the whole population without a job, only of those actively seeking. A person who stops looking because they believe no jobs are available is not counted as unemployed; they are out of the labour force entirely.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This is the number that makes headlines, but it hides underemployment and those too discouraged to search.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is the share of all adults who are actually employed. If LFPR is 60% and unemployment is 3%, then WPR is roughly 57%. So while LFPR includes jobseekers, WPR counts only those with jobs. It is a better measure of actual employment in the economy. For example, if a large number of people give up looking for work, LFPR drops, but WPR may not change much.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The gap between WPR and LFPR is the unemployment rate in percentage points. It shows how much of the workforce desire is unmet.

glossaryBlocks.term: NEET (Not in Employment, Education, or Training)
glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This refers to young people who are neither working, nor studying, nor learning any vocational skill. Imagine a 20-year-old who finished school but is not in college and not looking for a job, they are NEET. This group is at risk of long-term disadvantage because they are not gaining experience or qualifications. NEET rates are often higher for young women due to early marriage or household responsibilities.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India has one of the highest NEET shares globally. It captures youth disengagement that unemployment rates miss.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: These are workers hired on a daily or short-term basis without a written contract, regular salary, or benefits like paid leave or pension. A construction helper paid ₹400 for the day and then asked to come only when needed is a casual labourer. They have no job security and often no social safety net. Their earnings can be highly irregular.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India still has a large share of casual workers; they face the most precarious earnings and are often undercounted in surveys.

chartExplainers.title: India's labour force
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s labour force doubled from 30.8 crore in 1990 to 61.8 crore in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart traces the total number of Indians aged 15+ who are either working or actively looking for work. The upward slope is steady, reflecting both population growth and rising participation. The count includes everyone from farm labourers to software engineers, but it does not distinguish between those who have a job and those who are searching. The latest figure is 61.8 crore, up from 30.8 crore in 1990. Because the labour force includes the unemployed, this number is always larger than the number of people actually employed.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the headline count: how many Indians are in the workforce.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line rises from left to right; the rightmost point shows 61.8 crore.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don’t confuse it with the number of jobs, it includes unemployed jobseekers.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep the chart tall with a clear line and large data labels for quick reading.

chartExplainers.title: Labour force participation rate, 1991 to today
chartExplainers.takeaway: Only 55.7% of Indians aged 15+ were in the labour force in 2025, slightly below the 57.9% in 1990.
chartExplainers.detail: This line shows the labour force participation rate, the share of adults who are working or seeking work. It follows a shallow U-shape: starting at 57.9% in 1990, it declined to a low of around 52% in the mid-2000s, then recovered to 55.7% by 2025. A falling LFPR often means more people are studying, retiring, or staying home; the recent rise is partly driven by women entering the workforce. But at 55.7%, more than 4 out of 10 adults are still outside the labour force. The rate remains below the world average of 61%.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It answers: what share of working-age Indians are actually in the workforce.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line dips and then rises; the latest point is 55.7%.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A rise may not always be voluntary; distress can push people to seek work.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line chart with sparse x-axis labels to keep it clean.

chartExplainers.title: In the labour force vs actually working
chartExplainers.takeaway: The gap between LFPR (60.1%) and WPR (58.2%) in 2023-24 is the unemployment rate.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart overlays two closely related indicators: the Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR) and the Worker Population Ratio (WPR). LFPR includes both employed and unemployed jobseekers; WPR counts only those who have work. In 2023-24, LFPR was 60.1% and WPR was 58.2%, a gap of 1.9 percentage points. That gap equals the unemployment rate. In 2017-18, the gap was 3 points (49.8% vs 46.8%). The shrinking gap suggests that more people who want work are finding it, but the absolute rates have risen, meaning more adults are joining the labour force.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It makes the unemployment gap visually clear.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The two lines are close; the space between them is the unemployment rate.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: LFPR alone does not tell you how many are working; always check WPR.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use contrasting colors and label both lines on the chart.

chartExplainers.title: The unemployment rate over time
chartExplainers.takeaway: The unemployment rate has fallen from 7.6% in 1991 to 4.2% in 2025, but it understates joblessness.
chartExplainers.detail: This line tracks the share of the labour force that is without work but actively seeking it. It declined from a peak of 7.6% in 1991 to 4.2% in 2025, though with fluctuations. The rate spiked during the late 1990s and again after 2016, but has trended downward overall. However, many economists consider this an underestimate because it does not count discouraged workers or the underemployed. In India, a large section of the workforce is engaged in low-productivity self-employment, which masks the true employment challenge.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is the most widely reported jobs number, but needs context.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line shows a general downward slope from 1991 to 2025.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A low unemployment rate does not mean all workers have good jobs.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Annotate the start and end values so readers see the decline instantly.

chartExplainers.title: Men and women in the labour force
chartExplainers.takeaway: Women’s LFPR has jumped from 23.3% to 41.7% in six years, but it is still only half of men’s 78.8%.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart compares the LFPR for Indian men and women aged 15+ using PLFS data. Men’s participation has remained high and stable, rising slightly from 75.8% in 2017-18 to 78.8% in 2023-24. Women’s participation has shot up from 23.3% to 41.7%, an increase of 18.4 percentage points. This dramatic rise is one of the most debated labour market trends. Some attribute it to better measurement and genuinely improved work opportunities; others argue that economic distress pushed women into low-earning self-employment and casual farm work. The gap is still huge: for every 100 men in the workforce, there are only about 53 women.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The gender gap is the single most important fact about India’s workforce.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines: a high, flat line for men, and a steeply rising line for women.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The rise in women’s LFPR may not mean better jobs, just more women working out of necessity.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a clear legend and distinct colours; label key years.

chartExplainers.title: Youth struggle most to find work
chartExplainers.takeaway: Youth unemployment (10.2%) is three times the overall rate, and it affects young women slightly more than men.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows unemployment rates for young people aged 15–29, broken down by gender. The overall youth rate stood at 10.2% in 2023-24, with young men at 9.8% and young women at 11%. All three have fallen sharply from about 18% in 2017-18. But the rates remain alarmingly high, meaning one in ten young Indians who want a job cannot find one. Young women face a slightly higher rate, partly because they often search for specific types of work that are scarce. The 15–29 age band used in PLFS is broader than the global 15–24, so caution is needed when comparing with other countries.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Joblessness is concentrated among the young, not the whole workforce.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three lines start high and drop steeply; female line is usually the highest.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare directly with 15–24 youth rates from other countries without adjusting the age band.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a multi-line chart with thick lines and minimal clutter.

chartExplainers.title: The more educated, the higher the unemployment
chartExplainers.takeaway: Graduates face 13% unemployment, while those who are not literate have only 0.2%.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart stacks unemployment rates by education level. In 2023-24, the pattern is striking: ‘Not literate’ workers have an unemployment rate of just 0.2%, while graduates and postgraduates face 13% and 12.4%, respectively. Secondary-educated workers are in between at 1.9%. The low rate for the uneducated does not imply plenty of jobs; they simply cannot afford to remain jobless and take any work available. Graduates, on the other hand, often wait for salaried positions that match their qualifications. Over the years, rates have fallen for all groups, but the steep gradient persists.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is India’s paradox: higher education correlates with higher unemployment.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The lines fan out: the bottom line is near zero (not literate), the top lines are above 10% (graduates).
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don’t assume that low unemployment for less educated means abundant jobs; it means they cannot stay unemployed.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Label education groups clearly; use a compact legend.

chartExplainers.title: Unemployment by social group
chartExplainers.takeaway: Scheduled Tribes have the lowest unemployment (1.9%), while Others have the highest (3.8%).
chartExplainers.detail: This chart tracks unemployment rates for four social groups: Scheduled Castes (SC), Scheduled Tribes (ST), Other Backward Classes (OBC), and Others. In 2023-24, ST unemployment was 1.9%, SC 3.3%, OBC 3.1%, and Others 3.8%. All groups have seen a decline from rates of 4–7% in 2017-18. The lower unemployment for ST and some OBC groups may partly reflect higher rates of self-employment in agriculture and forestry, which conceals underemployment. The ‘Others’ category often includes more urban and educated workers, which aligns with the higher unemployment rates seen for graduates.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: To see whether joblessness varies by caste or tribe background.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Four lines declining over time; the ‘Others’ line is usually the top, ‘ST’ the bottom.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Low unemployment for ST does not mean good jobs; it may mean disguised unemployment in family labour.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use different colours and a clear legend; mark the latest year.

chartExplainers.title: How Indians are employed: the quality question
chartExplainers.takeaway: More than half of workers (58.4%) are self-employed, and regular wage jobs have shrunk to 21.7%.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the share of workers by employment status. Self-employment dominates and has grown from 52.2% in 2017-18 to 58.4% in 2023-24. Regular wage/salaried jobs, the most secure form, dipped from 22.8% to 21.7%. Casual labour fell from 24.9% to 19.8%, a positive shift. However, the rise in self-employment is not necessarily a sign of entrepreneurship; many own-account workers are in precarious trades like street vending or subsistence farming. The small and shrinking share of regular wage work underlines the scarcity of formal, stable employment.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Having a job does not mean having a good job; this breaks down the quality of work.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three stacked or multi-line series; the self-employment line is highest and rising.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Self-employment includes both millionaire entrepreneurs and hawkers; the chart cannot split them.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use an area chart or multi-line with thick strokes to show proportions.

chartExplainers.title: Where India works: farm, factory, office
chartExplainers.takeaway: Agriculture still employs 43.5% of workers, despite contributing much less to GDP.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart splits workers into three broad sectors: agriculture, industry, and services. In 2023-24, agriculture’s share was 43.5%, industry 24.9%, and services 31.6%. These shares have barely moved since 2017-18, when agriculture was 42.4% and services 32.6%. The sticky high farm employment indicates that many workers are stuck in low-productivity agriculture, unable to shift to manufacturing or services at scale. Economic shocks can even reverse the transition, pushing people back to farms. A large agricultural workforce often points to hidden underemployment.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows the structure of work across the economy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three lines; agriculture stays near the top, services middle, industry bottom.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Farm employment share can rise during crises; that does not mean agriculture is booming.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a simple multi-line, avoid stacking if it compresses the chart.

chartExplainers.title: How India's participation compares
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s LFPR (55.7%) is lower than Vietnam, Indonesia, China, and the world average.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart compares India’s LFPR with Asian peers and the world. In 2025, Vietnam had the highest at 72.8%, followed by Indonesia (68%), China (64.6%), Bangladesh (58.8%), and India (55.7%). The world average was 61%. India’s rate has declined slightly from 57.9% in 1990, while Bangladesh’s has risen. One visible pattern in this data is the low participation of women. In Vietnam and Indonesia, female LFPR is much higher, pulling up the overall rate. Bangladesh is closer to India, also with low female participation, though recent gains in the garment industry have boosted it. These comparisons highlight that India’s workforce participation is not just about economic growth but also about social norms and access to acceptable work.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: To place India’s workforce participation in a global context.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Multiple lines; India’s line is near the bottom, Vietnam’s near the top.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Comparisons are complicated by different age ranges and measurement methods.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a clear legend outside the chart area to keep it readable.

chartExplainers.title: Young Indians not in work, education or training
chartExplainers.takeaway: 24.2% of young Indians are NEET, down from 32.6% in 1994 but still among the highest globally.
chartExplainers.detail: This line shows the share of youth aged 15–24 who are not in employment, education, or training. The rate has fallen from 32.6% in 1994 to 24.2% in 2024, a decline of 8.4 percentage points over three decades. Still, one in four young people are disengaged from both earning and learning. Many are young women doing unpaid household work. The NEET rate is a broader gauge of youth vulnerability than unemployment because it captures those who have stopped looking for work. India’s NEET share is one of the highest in the world, signaling a large pool of untapped potential.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It measures the truly disengaged youth whom the unemployment rate misses.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line drifts downward from 32% to 24% over 30 years.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: NEET includes those doing care work; it does not mean they are idle by choice.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Annotate the start and end values prominently.

chartExplainers.title: Hiring momentum, month by month
chartExplainers.takeaway: The Naukri JobSpeak index shows formal hiring has grown from 902 to 2,836 since 2008, with sharp pandemic dips.
chartExplainers.detail: This monthly index aggregates online job postings on Naukri.com. It tripled from 902 in August 2008 to 2,836 in May 2026, indicating a long-term expansion in formal-sector hiring demand. The series is volatile, with visible drops during the 2020 COVID lockdown and the 2016 demonetisation. Because it reflects corporate and IT hiring, it provides a real-time pulse of the organised job market, complementing dated official surveys. However, it over-represents urban, educated, and IT/BPO roles, and misses hiring in the informal sector, which employs most Indians.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It offers a faster read on formal hiring than annual government surveys.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Check the long-term upward trend and the sharp V-shaped recovery after dips.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: It does not represent the entire job market; rural and informal hiring is absent.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line with shaded areas for dips; label peak and trough values.

chartExplainers.title: Demand for rural guaranteed work
chartExplainers.takeaway: NREGA person-days peaked in 2013 and spiked during COVID, but have since moderated to 109.93 million in April 2026.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots monthly person-days of work generated under MGNREGA, India’s rural employment guarantee scheme. The series shows strong seasonal patterns, with work concentrated in dry months. Person-days were highest in April 2013 at 186.84 million, then declined until the COVID-19 pandemic caused a huge surge as migrant workers returned home. Since 2020, demand has moderated but stays elevated. High NREGA demand often signals distress in rural labour markets, but it can also reflect better implementation and awareness. It serves as a critical safety net when other work options vanish.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: NREGA demand acts as a barometer of rural job distress.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for spikes; the highest bars are in summer months and pandemic years.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: High demand is not pure unemployment; it also depends on government implementation and fund releases.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a bar chart or line with seasonal labels; avoid too many bars on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: What different kinds of work pay
chartExplainers.takeaway: Regular workers earn ₹20,702/month, self-employed ₹13,279/month, while casual labourers get ₹418/day but few guaranteed workdays.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart compares average earnings across three employment types. Regular wage/salaried workers earned ₹20,702 per month in 2023-24, up from ₹16,527. Self-employed workers averaged ₹13,279 per month, up from ₹12,029. Casual labourers got ₹418 per day, up from ₹256. But casual workers face the most uncertainty: ₹418 a day means little if work is available only 10 days a month. The wide gap between regular and casual earnings illustrates the stark inequality in India’s labour market. Regular jobs, besides higher pay, usually come with benefits and job security that the other categories lack.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Earnings reveal the real economic divide between different kinds of work.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Note the different units: monthly for regular and self-employed, daily for casual; compare the growth trends.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don’t multiply casual daily wage by 30; actual monthly income is much lower due to few workdays.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a dual-axis chart if needed; label axes clearly.

sourceNotes: World Bank: Labour force and participation estimates are modelled by the ILO using national labour force surveys and other sources.
Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI): Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) provides annual data for India for 2017-18 to 2023-24.
Naukri JobSpeak index: Naukri.com monthly hiring index via India Data Hub. Reflects formal sector job listings.
MGNREGA person-days: Ministry of Rural Development data via India Data Hub. Monthly person-days of work created under the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act.
India Data Hub aggregates these high-frequency indicators.
caveats: The official unemployment rate counts only those actively seeking work; it excludes discouraged workers and the underemployed.
Self-employment includes both entrepreneurs and vulnerable own-account workers; the category is highly heterogeneous.
NREGA demand reflects both economic distress and administrative capacity; it is not a pure indicator of unemployment.
JobSpeak covers only the organised sector and skews toward IT/BPO and urban white-collar roles, missing informal hiring.
Cross-country comparisons of LFPR are affected by differences in age cohorts, measurement methods, and treatment of informal work.
The PLFS youth age band (15–29) is broader than the global standard (15–24), so direct comparisons need care.
A rise in women’s LFPR may partly reflect distress-driven work rather than better employment opportunities.
Lower unemployment for some marginalised groups may mask underemployment or forced self-employment.

chartPlan.title: India's labour force
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The headline count: how many Indians are working or looking for work.
chartPlan.howToRead: Read the latest value and trace the growth trend from 1990 to 2025.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse with the number of people actually employed; this includes those seeking work.

chartPlan.title: Labour force participation rate, 1991 to today
chartPlan.whyShowThis: What share of working-age Indians are in the labour force at all.
chartPlan.howToRead: Note the level (about 55-58%) and the U-shaped pattern over decades.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A rising rate may be driven by women entering the workforce out of distress, not just opportunity.

chartPlan.title: In the labour force vs actually working
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The gap between wanting work and having it is unemployment.
chartPlan.howToRead: See that WPR is a few points below LFPR; the difference is the unemployment rate.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume LFPR equals the share with jobs; it includes job seekers.

chartPlan.title: The unemployment rate over time
chartPlan.whyShowThis: How hard it is to find work, the number people feel most.
chartPlan.howToRead: Track how the rate fell from 7.6% in 1991 to 4.2% in 2025, but with fluctuations.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Low official unemployment can hide deep underemployment and low-quality work.

chartPlan.title: Men and women in the labour force
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The single biggest fact about India's workforce: the gender gap.
chartPlan.howToRead: Men's LFPR is stable near 78%, women's LFPR has risen sharply from 23% to 42% in the PLFS years.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The rise in female LFPR may partly reflect improved measurement and distress-push, not necessarily empowerment.

chartPlan.title: Youth struggle most to find work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Joblessness is concentrated among the young, not the whole workforce.
chartPlan.howToRead: Note that youth unemployment is two to three times the overall rate, and has come down but remains high.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Combining young men and women can mask differences; the chart shows both.

chartPlan.title: The more educated, the higher the unemployment
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India's paradox: unemployment rises with education, not falls.
chartPlan.howToRead: The not literate have near-zero unemployment; graduates and postgraduates face rates above 12%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Low unemployment among the less educated often means they cannot afford to be without any work, not that jobs are abundant.

chartPlan.title: Unemployment by social group
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Whether finding work depends on who you are.
chartPlan.howToRead: Compare the rates for SC, ST, OBC, and Others; all have declined, but gaps persist.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Lower unemployment for some groups may reflect higher rates of disguised self-employment rather than true job access.

chartPlan.title: How Indians are employed: the quality question
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Having work is not the same as having a good job.
chartPlan.howToRead: Self-employment dominates and has grown; regular wage jobs are stuck near 22%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Self-employment ranges from prosperous entrepreneurs to subsistence own-account workers; the chart cannot split them.

chartPlan.title: Where India works: farm, factory, office
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The structure of work across the economy.
chartPlan.howToRead: Agriculture still holds the largest share at over 43%, with services next and industry stable.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Farm employment share can rise during economic shocks as people fall back on agriculture, even as they want non-farm jobs.

chartPlan.title: How India's participation compares
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India's participation against its peers and the world.
chartPlan.howToRead: India's LFPR is lower than Indonesia, Vietnam, and China, and below the world average; only Bangladesh is similar.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Low LFPR is partly due to low female participation; comparisons should consider demographic and cultural differences.

chartPlan.title: Young Indians not in work, education or training
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The disengaged young, neither learning nor earning.
chartPlan.howToRead: One in four young Indians is NEET; the rate has fallen from 33% to 24% over 30 years but remains high.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: NEET includes those doing unpaid care work; it does not mean they are idle by choice.

chartPlan.title: Hiring momentum, month by month
chartPlan.whyShowThis: A real-time read on formal hiring, faster than annual surveys.
chartPlan.howToRead: The index shows long-term growth (from 902 to 2,836) with sharp pandemic dips and recoveries.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: It covers only the organised sector and skews toward IT/BPO; it misses small firms and informal hiring.

chartPlan.title: Demand for rural guaranteed work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: When other work dries up, rural Indians fall back on the NREGA jobs guarantee.
chartPlan.howToRead: Person-days spiked during the pandemic and have since moderated; seasonal patterns are sharp.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: High NREGA demand can reflect both distress and better implementation; it is not a pure indicator of unemployment.

chartPlan.title: What different kinds of work pay
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Earnings vary hugely by the kind of work.
chartPlan.howToRead: Regular wage workers earn about ₹20,702/month, self-employed ₹13,279/month, while casual labourers get ₹418/day but may not find full-month work.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Casual daily wages cannot be directly multiplied by 30 to get monthly earnings; work days are limited.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: Yes, the selected data points cover the main dimensions of workforce size, participation, unemployment, gender, age, education, social groups, employment quality, sectors, international comparison, and high-frequency indicators. The PLFS data points are sufficient for recent trends, and the World Bank series provide long-term context.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Think of it as the pool of people who are either working or actively looking for work. It includes the employed and the unemployed, but not those who have stopped searching or are not interested in working.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: This page tracks how big this pool is and who is in it, which is the starting point for understanding jobs in India.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: If you take everyone aged 15 and above, what percentage are in the labour force? So if LFPR is 55%, it means 55 out of 100 working-age people are either employed or seeking work. The other 45 are out of the workforce, they might be students, homemakers, retired, or discouraged.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It tells us how much of India’s potential workers are actually joining the workforce. A falling LFPR can mean more people are quitting job search.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Of those in the labour force, the ones who want to work, what percentage cannot find a job? It is calculated as (unemployed ÷ labour force) × 100. A rate of 3% means that out of 100 people in the labour force, 3 are jobless and looking for work.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: This is the number that makes headlines, but it hides underemployment and those too discouraged to search.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: This is the share of the total adult population that is actually employed. Unlike LFPR, it does not include job seekers. So WPR is always equal to or lower than LFPR.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The gap between WPR and LFPR is the unemployment rate in percentage points. It shows how much of the workforce desire is unmet.

glossaryPlan.term: NEET (Not in Employment, Education, or Training)
glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Young people (usually 15–24 or 15–29) who are neither working nor studying nor receiving any vocational training. They are disconnected from both earning and learning.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India has one of the highest NEET shares globally. It captures youth disengagement that unemployment rates miss.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Workers hired on a daily or intermittent basis without a regular contract or social security benefits. They are paid by the day, and work is uncertain.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India still has a large share of casual workers; they face the most precarious earnings and are often undercounted in surveys.

plannedCharts.title: India's labour force
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline count: how many Indians are working or looking for work.

plannedCharts.title: Labour force participation rate, 1991 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: What share of working-age Indians are in the labour force at all.

plannedCharts.title: In the labour force vs actually working
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The gap between wanting work and having it is unemployment.

plannedCharts.title: The unemployment rate over time
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: How hard it is to find work, the number people feel most.

plannedCharts.title: Men and women in the labour force
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single biggest fact about India's workforce: the gender gap.

plannedCharts.title: Youth struggle most to find work
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Joblessness is concentrated among the young, not the whole workforce.

plannedCharts.title: The more educated, the higher the unemployment
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's paradox: unemployment rises with education, not falls.

plannedCharts.title: Unemployment by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether finding work depends on who you are.

plannedCharts.title: How Indians are employed: the quality question
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Having work is not the same as having a good job.

plannedCharts.title: Where India works: farm, factory, office
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The structure of work across the economy.

plannedCharts.title: How India's participation compares
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's participation against its peers and the world.

plannedCharts.title: Young Indians not in work, education or training
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The disengaged young, neither learning nor earning.

plannedCharts.title: Hiring momentum, month by month
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A real-time read on formal hiring, faster than annual surveys.

plannedCharts.title: Demand for rural guaranteed work
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: When other work dries up, rural Indians fall back on the NREGA jobs guarantee.

plannedCharts.title: What different kinds of work pay
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Earnings vary hugely by the kind of work.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
